expectations.text: Output file is a duplicate (not the original) — original 02-portfolio-minimal.html is untouched
expectations.evidence: Original file is 99 lines with no script tags or animation CSS. Output file is 539 lines with GSAP scripts and animation code. The two files differ significantly, confirming the original was not modified.

expectations.text: GSAP core + ScrollTrigger CDN scripts are included via script tags
SplitText plugin is loaded from cdn.jsdelivr.net and registered
expectations.evidence: No SplitText plugin is loaded anywhere in the output. Instead, custom utility functions wrapLines() and splitWords() are used (lines 162-187). No reference to cdn.jsdelivr.net or SplitText appears in the file.

expectations.text: Hero has a GSAP timeline with 4+ chained .fromTo or .from calls
expectations.evidence: The hero timeline (heroTL, line 228) has only 2 chained calls: heroTL.to(heroLineInners, ...) and heroTL.to(subtitleWordInners, ..., '-=0.6'). Both use .to() not .fromTo() or .from(), and the count is 2, not 4+.

expectations.text: gsap.matchMedia wraps animation code with prefers-reduced-motion guard
expectations.evidence: Lines 533-536 use window.matchMedia('(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)') with a plain if-check that kills animations after the fact. The expectation requires gsap.matchMedia() wrapping the animation code, which is GSAP's recommended API for responsive/accessible animations. The output uses the browser's native API as a post-hoc kill switch instead.

expectations.text: autoAlpha is used instead of bare opacity in gsap.from/fromTo calls
expectations.evidence: autoAlpha is used consistently throughout all GSAP calls (lines 195, 196, 276, 300, 302, 303, 367, 416, 436, 437, 448, 458, 495, 496, 507, 517). No bare 'opacity' property appears in any GSAP tween.

expectations.text: ScrollTrigger scroll reveals cover projects, about, and contact sections
expectations.evidence: Projects: ScrollTrigger on projects-header (line 283) and each project-item (line 307). About: ScrollTrigger on about label (line 373), about text (line 391), capabilities label (line 407), skills list (line 424). Contact: ScrollTrigger on contact timeline (line 441).

expectations.text: At least one interactive effect exists (magnetic, spring hover, or tilt)
expectations.evidence: Lines 467-486 implement a magnetic hover effect on the contact email: mousemove tracks cursor position and shifts element proportionally (x * 0.15, y * 0.2), mouseleave snaps back with elastic.out(1, 0.4) easing. Also, project items have a hover shift effect (lines 348-356).

expectations.text: No CSS transition conflicts with GSAP-owned properties
expectations.evidence: The original CSS has 'nav .links a{...transition:opacity .3s ease}' (line 19 of output). GSAP animates autoAlpha (which controls opacity) on nav links (lines 196, 207-213). The CSS transition on opacity can interfere with GSAP's tween of the same property. The output did not remove or override this CSS transition.

expectations.text: Token comments are present (e.g. // token: base, // token: entrance)
expectations.evidence: No '// token:' comments appear anywhere in the output JavaScript. The comments present are section headers like '// 1. NAV — Fade in from top', '// 2. HERO — Kinetic typography', etc. Token-based comments identifying animation categories (base, entrance, etc.) are entirely absent.

expectations.text: gsap.registerPlugin() is called for all used plugins
expectations.evidence: Line 157: gsap.registerPlugin(ScrollTrigger). ScrollTrigger is the only GSAP plugin loaded and used in the output.

expectations.text: ScrollTrigger.refresh() is called on window load event

claims.claim: Used autoAlpha (visibility + opacity) instead of bare opacity throughout
claims.evidence: All GSAP fade animations use autoAlpha, confirmed by searching the output. No bare opacity in any GSAP call.
Lines 533-536 check window.matchMedia('(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)') and kill animations. However, it uses the browser API rather than gsap.matchMedia().

claims.claim: Kinetic typography with scroll-linked horizontal parallax
claims.evidence: Lines 239-250 create per-line parallax with alternating +60px/-60px horizontal shift, scrubbed at 0.8 on scroll past hero.

claims.claim: No FLIP, no 3D transforms, no color changes
claims.evidence: Searched entire JS section: no rotateX/Y/Z, perspective, or FLIP references. Only 2D transforms (x, y, scale, rotate) and autoAlpha are used.

claims.claim: Magnetic hover effect on contact email CTA
claims.evidence: Lines 467-486 implement proportional mouse-following on the email element with elastic snap-back on mouse leave.

claims.claim: All animations are self-contained in a single HTML file
claims.evidence: The output is a single .html file with inline CSS and JS. External dependencies are only the Google Fonts and GSAP CDN scripts.

suggestions.assertion: No CSS transition conflicts with GSAP-owned properties
suggestions.reason: This assertion is useful but hard to grade definitively. The nav links opacity transition is technically a conflict but unlikely to cause visible issues since GSAP overwrites per-frame. Consider tightening the assertion to check whether the output actively removes or overrides pre-existing CSS transitions on GSAP-animated properties, rather than just checking for absence of conflict.

suggestions.assertion: SplitText plugin is loaded from cdn.jsdelivr.net and registered
suggestions.reason: This assertion is overly prescriptive about implementation. The output achieves text splitting with custom utility functions that work correctly. A better assertion might test whether text splitting functionality exists (regardless of method), or keep this assertion specifically as a skill-differentiator if SplitText usage is a key skill requirement.

No assertion checks whether the output HTML is valid and renders without JavaScript errors. The custom splitWords() function naively splits on whitespace including inside HTML tags like <strong>, which could break markup. An assertion verifying structural HTML validity of the animated output would catch this class of bugs.

No assertion checks whether the hero kinetic typography actually produces a visually compelling effect. The transcript claims 'scroll-linked horizontal parallax' but this could range from barely noticeable to overwhelming. A visual regression or screenshot comparison assertion would add genuine quality verification.

eval_feedback.overall: The eval set is reasonably comprehensive for checking GSAP best practices. The main gap is that several assertions test for specific implementation patterns from the motion-dev skill (SplitText, token comments, gsap.matchMedia, 4+ chained .fromTo) that a baseline run without the skill would naturally miss. This is actually desirable for a with/without skill comparison — these are the assertions that should discriminate. The 58% baseline pass rate suggests good discriminating power.
